Titulo: Putting ChatGPT vision (GPT-4V) to the test: risk perception in traffic images
Autores: Tom Driessen, Dimitra Dodou, P. Bazilinskyy, J. D. de Winter
Introduccion: Vision-language models are of interest in various domains, including automated driving, where computer vision techniques can accurately detect road users, but where the vehicle sometimes fails to understand context.
Analisis_Requerimientos: The findings suggest that GPT-4V must be prompted in a way equivalent to how humans fill out a multi-item questionnaire.
Discusiones: Based on psychometric construct theory and using insights from the self-consistency prompting method, we formulated three hypotheses: (i) repeating the prompt under effectively identical conditions increases validity, (ii) varying the prompt text and extracting a total score increases validity compared to using a single prompt, and (iii) in a multiple regression analysis, the incorporation of object detection features, alongside the GPT-4V-based risk rating, significantly contributes to improving the model's validity.
Conclusion: GPT-4V, a vision-language model, accurately predicts human risk in traffic images when prompted in a way similar to how humans fill out multi-item questionnaires.

Titulo: PVswin-YOLOv8s: UAV-Based Pedestrian and Vehicle Detection for Traffic Management in Smart Cities Using Improved YOLOv8
Autores: Noor Ul Ain Tahir, Zhe Long, Zuping Zhang, M. Asim, Mohammed Elaffendi
Introduccion: In smart cities, effective traffic congestion management hinges on adept pedestrian and vehicle detection. Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) offer a solution with mobility, cost-effectiveness, and a wide field of view, and yet, optimizing recognition models is crucial to surmounting challenges posed by small and occluded objects.
Metodologia: YOLOv8, YOLOv5, YOLOv3, YOLO, RetinaNet, CNN (Redes Convolucionales), Transformer
Limitaciones: Finally, we employ Soft-NMS to improve the accuracy of pedestrian and vehicle detection in occlusion situations. Soft-NMS increases performance and manages overlapped boundary boxes well.
Discusiones: Performance comparisons with different YOLO versions ( for example YOLOv3 extremely small, YOLOv5, YOLOv6, and YOLOv7), YOLOv8 variants (YOLOv8n, YOLOv8s, YOLOv8m, and YOLOv8l), and classical object detectors (Faster-RCNN, Cascade R-CNN, RetinaNet, and CenterNet) were used to validate the superiority of the proposed PVswin-YOLOv8s model. The efficiency of the PVswin-YOLOv8s model was confirmed by the experimental findings, which showed a 4.8% increase in average detection accuracy (mAP) compared to YOLOv8s on the VisDrone2019 dataset. The PVswin-YOLOv8s model improves pedestrian and vehicle detection in smart cities by incorporating the Swin Transformer model, CBAM, and Soft-NMS, leading to a 4.8% increase in average detection accuracy compared to YOLOv8s.
Conclusion: The PVswin-YOLOv8s model improves pedestrian and vehicle detection in smart cities by incorporating the Swin Transformer model, CBAM, and Soft-NMS, leading to a 4.8% increase in average detection accuracy compared to YOLOv8s.

Discusiones: The experimental results show that the fine-tuned AlexNet outperforms other models providing an 80% percent true positive rate when detecting anomalies (accidents) in real-world surveillance videos of UCF-Crime dataset.
Conclusion: Manually generated fake accident data can effectively train machine learning algorithms to detect real-world road accidents in surveillance videos, with an 80% true positive rate for AlexNet.

Analisis_Requerimientos: Through the analysis and comparison of experimental results, it is proved that the proposed BV-YOLOv5S network model performs better and is more reliable in the detection of pavement defects and can meet the needs of road safety detection projects with high real-time and flexibility requirements.
Discusiones: In the evaluation test of the model in the constructed PCD1 (Pavement Check Dataset) dataset, the mAP@.5 (mean Average Precision when IoU = 0.5) of the BV-YOLOv5S (BiFPN Varifocal Loss-YOLOv5S) model increased by 4.1%, 3%, and 0.9%, respectively, compared with the YOLOv3-tiny, YOLOv5S, and B-YOLOv5S (BiFPN-YOLOv5S; BV-YOLOv5S does not use the Improved Focal Loss function) models.
Conclusion: The proposed BV-YOLOv5S network model, combining the YOLO algorithm with BIFPN and Varifocal Loss, improves pavement defect detection accuracy and reliability in real-time and flexible road safety projects.
